Commission Implementing Regulation (EU) 2025/1706 of 25 July 2025 — one of the first implementing acts under the Euro 7 Regulation (EU) 2024/1257 — was published in the Official Journal, laying down test methodologies for exhaust and evaporative emission type-approval of M1/N1 vehicles ('Euro 7 - TEMP'). It entered into force 25 September 2025.

EPA published a proposed rule (signed 29 July 2025) to rescind the 2009 Greenhouse Gas Endangerment Finding and repeal all GHG emission standards for light-, medium- and heavy-duty motor vehicles and engines, arguing the Clean Air Act does not authorize such standards. The comment period was later extended to 22 September 2025.

The Road Vehicles (Type-Approval) (Amendment) Regulations 2025 (SI 2025 No. 661), laid before Parliament 12 June 2025, entered into force on 3 July 2025. They make EU VECTO component certifications permanently acceptable in the GB scheme and add updated UN Regulations for ISOFIX and Automated Lane Keeping Systems (ALKS).

Australia's New Vehicle Efficiency Standard (NVES) commenced 1 January 2025 with mandatory CO2 targets for new-vehicle suppliers (141 g/km headline for passenger cars/SUVs, 210 g/km for larger Type 2 vehicles). The penalty component took effect 1 July 2025, charging AUD 100 per gram of excess emissions, with a unit-trading scheme.

At its 196th session (24-27 June 2025), the UNECE World Forum WP.29 considered amendments to UN Regulation No. 171 on Driver Control Assistance Systems (DCAS), a Level-2 ADAS framework, plus modifications across dozens of other UN vehicle regulations covering safety, lighting and steering equipment.

Regulation (EU) 2025/1214 (adopted 17 June 2025) amends the CO2 emission-performance standards for cars and vans (Reg. (EU) 2019/631) to let manufacturers comply over a 2025-2027 three-year average rather than annually — easing the 2025 target after weaker-than-expected EV demand.

NHTSA issued the Third Amended Standing General Order on crash reporting for Automated Driving Systems and Level 2 ADAS on 24 April 2025 (effective 16 June 2025), part of its new AV Framework, reducing duplicative reporting and refining reportable-incident thresholds.

On 12 March 2025 EPA announced it would reconsider the Multi-Pollutant Emissions Standards for Model Year 2027-and-later light- and medium-duty vehicles (finalized March 2024), reopening the GHG and criteria-pollutant standards that were to phase in across MY2027-2032.

The European Commission presented its Industrial Action Plan for the automotive sector on 5 March 2025, including the proposal to add CO2-compliance flexibility (three-year averaging for 2025-2027), a European Connected and Autonomous Vehicle Alliance, and battery-manufacturing support.

On 3 March 2025 Japan's MLIT study group published its final report on measures to ensure the safety performance of automated vehicles, proposing scenario-based tests requiring AVs to exceed a 'competent and careful human driver' and In-Service Monitoring and Reporting (ISMR) by manufacturers after launch.

From 1 February 2025 it is no longer possible to apply for Provisional GB type approval for M, N and O category vehicles, a step in the transition to the full GB Type Approval scheme that becomes mandatory for cars and goods vehicles sold in Great Britain from February 2026.

NHTSA delayed the effective date of the FMVSS No. 127 light-vehicle automatic-emergency-braking final rule (published 26 November 2024) to 20 March 2025 and granted portions of industry petitions for reconsideration, following a 20 January 2025 presidential regulatory-review memorandum.

In January 2025 NHTSA issued an NPRM proposing the ADS-equipped Vehicle Safety, Transparency, and Evaluation Program (AV STEP), a voluntary national program under which participants submit a safety case and consent to independent assessment and ongoing oversight of automated-driving operations.
